turns.text:
I'll build a worksheet grounded in the DDPM & DDIM notes you've attached — covering forward process, reverse process, sampling, and the key formulas.
<function_calls>
<invoke name="generate_assessment">
<parameter name="type">assessment</parameter>
<parameter name="title">DDPM & DDIM Diffusion Models — Practice Worksheet</parameter>
<parameter name="description">A worksheet for advanced students covering DDPM and DDIM core concepts: noise schedules, forward and reverse processes, posterior distributions, training losses, and sampling strategies (DDPM vs. DDIM vs. Improved DDPM). Questions emphasize formula derivation, parameter relationships, and practical distinctions between sampling methods. Grounded in 06_diffusion_ddpm_ddim_notes.pdf.</parameter>
<parameter name="params">{
  "topic": "DDPM & DDIM Diffusion Models",
  "assessment_type": "worksheet",
  "question_count": 12,
  "question_types": ["short_answer", "fill_blank"],
  "level": "advanced",
  "language": "en"
}</parameter>
</invoke>
</function_calls>

Here's a 12-question worksheet — short-answer and fill-in-the-blank — grounded in your DDPM & DDIM notes. It covers noise schedules, forward/reverse processes, posterior distributions, training losses, and the key distinctions between DDPM, DDIM, and Improved DDPM sampling. You can tweak the difficulty, add more questions, or refocus any section on the card.

turns.prompt: add a small hint under each problem
turns.text:
<function_calls>
<invoke name="refine_assessment">
<parameter name="instruction">Add a small, one-line hint beneath each of the 6 problems. Hints should point toward the relevant formula, concept, or approach without giving away the answer. For example, hints like 'Think about the product of α_i terms', 'Use the closed-form distribution', 'SNR relates signal to noise variance', etc.</parameter>
<parameter name="summary">Added one-line hints under each problem</parameter>
</invoke>
</function_calls>

On it — adding a one-line hint under each problem to guide students toward the right formula or concept without spoiling the answer. Updating the worksheet now.
